title: Chapter 64: Taipei
content: The C-130 hit the runway so hard Marcus's teeth rattled in his skull.

Colonel Vance had called the approach "sporty." That was military understatement for a combat landing on a cracked runway at Taipei Taoyuan International Airport, which hadn't seen a controlled aircraft in seven months. The port engine whined as reverse thrust kicked in, and Marcus watched through the tiny porthole as the world outside decelerated from blur to nightmare.

Taoyuan was a graveyard of aviation. A China Airlines 747 sat bisected across Taxiway Alpha, its fuselage torn open like a gutted fish. Baggage carts lay overturned on the tarmac, their contents scattered—suitcases burst open, clothes and duty-free shopping bags moldering in the tropical humidity. The terminal itself was a burned-out husk, its glass curtain wall shattered, dark gaps staring at them like hollow eye sockets.

But the runway — Runway 05 Right, specifically — was intact enough. Cracked, yeah. Weeds pushing through the expansion joints. A shallow crater about two thousand feet down that Vance had catalogued from satellite imagery and assured them the C-130 could hop. But intact enough.
"Thirty seconds!" Vance shouted from the cockpit.

Marcus checked his gear for the fifth time. Plate carrier, snug. Magazine pouches, full. The Sig Sauer MCX racked and ready on safe. Two fragmentation grenades on his left hip. The katana — Sera's katana, the one she'd pressed into his hands before this mission with a look that said *don't you dare die before I can kill you myself* — secured across his back.
He caught his reflection in the porthole glass. Tactical gear, scarred jaw, eyes that had seen too much. He barely recognized himself some days.
The C-130 lurched to a stop.
"Go, go, go!"

The cargo ramp dropped and the Baroness's mercenaries were first out — eight professionals in black tactical gear who moved like a single organism, fanning into a perimeter with the fluid precision of people who'd done this exact thing in countries that didn't officially exist. Their leader, a Hungarian named Kovacs who had cheekbones that could cut glass and precisely zero sense of humor, gave the all-clear hand sign.

Marcus followed. The humidity hit him like a wall. Taiwan in late October was still sweltering, the air thick with moisture and the sweet-rotten smell of vegetation reclaiming concrete, overlaid with something worse — the distinctive organic decay that meant necrotic infection had been through here. Recently.
"Contact left!" one of the mercs barked.

They came from the terminal. A stream of them — former travelers and airport staff, still wearing the tattered remains of uniforms and vacation clothes, their skin gone the grey-purple of advanced necrosis. Twenty, maybe thirty, shambling across the tarmac with that horrible mechanical persistence that Marcus had learned to fear more than speed.
He brought the MCX up.

The first three dropped to controlled bursts. Double-tap. Move. Double-tap. Sera's voice in his head: *Don't think of them as people. They're not. They're projectiles with teeth. Treat them like incoming rounds — you don't debate incoming rounds, you move and you put them down.*

The mercenaries were efficient. No wasted ammunition, no panic fire. Kovacs dropped three with a precision that was almost surgical, each shot placement deliberate — center mass first, then a follow-up to the cranial cavity. The Baroness paid for the best, and the best were worth every round.
"Push through!" Marcus ordered. "Terminal's the chokepoint. We clear it or we die in it."

They moved as a unit, leapfrogging through the terminal's shattered entrance. Inside was worse. The departure hall was a charnel house of overturned benches and abandoned luggage, and the infected had been nesting. They came from behind check-in counters, from duty-free shops, from the skeletal frames of security checkpoints. A woman in a Singapore Airlines uniform lunged from behind a pillar and Marcus put two rounds through her chest before she got within arm's reach.

The fighting was close, ugly, personal. One of the mercenaries — a French-Canadian named Dufresne — took a bite through his glove and kept fighting with his off-hand until Kovacs put the infected down and flagged the wound for quarantine later. That was the deal the Baroness had made with her people: anyone bitten stayed in isolation until they either survived the infection or didn't. No exceptions. No mercy killings until it was necessary.

It took forty minutes to clear the terminal. Forty minutes of methodical, room-by-room clearing that left Marcus's ears ringing and his magazine pouches noticeably lighter. Seventeen infected confirmed down. No casualties among the living.
Marcus gave the order to move out.
The drive into central Taipei took two hours.

Two hours of carefully navigating the Xinyi Expressway — or what remained of it. Abandoned vehicles formed a canyon of rusted metal that the convoy's modified trucks had to thread through like a needle. The infected were thinner here, most of the city's remaining horde having migrated south toward the warmer climate of Tainan months ago.
Through the truck's open window, Marcus watched his parents' city swim into focus.

He'd grown up in this skyline. Had memorized every tower from the back seat of his father's car on the way to cram school. Now the buildings stood like broken teeth. The Far Eastern Plaza had lost its top fifteen floors to what looked like an internal explosion. The Neo19 mall was a blackened shell. But ahead, rising above the devastation like a middle finger to the apocalypse, stood Taipei 101.

The former supertall was still standing. More than standing — it had been *fortified*. The lower floors were sealed with concrete barriers and welded steel plate. Gun emplacements — crude but functional — protruded from windows on the 15th and 30th floors. Solar panels covered the upper facade, angled to catch sunlight above the haze line. Someone had painted enormous Chinese characters across the 60th-floor exterior: 存活 — *Survive*.
Marcus's throat tightened.
"Convoy, this is Chen," he said into the radio. "Approaching the structure. Do not — repeat, do not — engage unless engaged. These are friendlies."
Static. Then a voice, cracked with disbelief:
"This is Taipei 101. Chen? Marcus Chen?"
"That's affirmative."
A pause. Shouting in the background — Mandarin and Taiwanese both, overlapping.
"...Marcus, your mother is going to kill you."

The 89th floor had been converted into a living space. Makeshift partitions divided the open plan into sleeping quarters, a medical area, a communal kitchen. The observation deck windows that had once offered tourists a panoramic view of one of Asia's greatest cities now looked out over a world of ruins and silence.

Forty-three survivors. Men, women, four children under twelve. They watched Marcus and his team file off the elevator with expressions that hovered between hope and terror — hope because new people meant news, supplies, maybe a way out; terror because new people also meant noise, and noise brought the dead.
His mother was standing near the far window, sorting medical supplies into labeled bins.

She was thinner than he remembered. Her hair, once jet black, was streaked with grey. She wore a faded cardigan over a thermal shirt, and her hands — the hands that had made him thousand-layer scallion pancakes every birthday, that had smoothed his hair when he couldn't sleep — shook slightly as she worked.
She looked up at the sound of boots on concrete.

Her eyes passed over him. Over the tactical gear, the weapons, the hard lines of a face that had been soft and pale just eighteen months ago. Passed over all of it without recognition, because the boy she was looking for — the gamer who wouldn't leave his room, who argued about raid schedules, who she had to threaten with disconnected WiFi to get to the dinner table — that boy was nowhere visible in the warlord standing in her refuge.
Then she saw his eyes.
Just his eyes. Brown and wide and exactly the same as they'd always been.
"Marcus?"

The word came out like a wound. She moved — didn't walk, *launched* — across the room, and her arms were around him before he could set down his rifle. She held him with a ferocity that made his body armor creak, fingers digging into the plate carrier like she was trying to reach through it to his skin, and she was saying his name over and over, *Marcus, Marcus, Marcus*, like a prayer she'd been saving for exactly this moment.

He couldn't speak. He couldn't breathe. Every wall he'd built — the warlord mask, the tactical training, the cold efficiency that had kept him alive — crumbled like wet paper. He was thirteen again, waking from a nightmare, and his mother was there, and everything was going to be okay.
"Mom," he managed. Just that. Just the word he hadn't said aloud in seven months.

She pulled back, cupped his face in her hands — her small, shaking, beautiful hands — and studied every inch of him. Her thumb traced the scar along his jaw. Her eyes catalogued the damage. She didn't cry. Mei-Ling Chen had never been a crier. But her expression held so much that tears would have been redundant.
"You're so thin," she said. "You were always thin, but this — have you been eating?"
He laughed. An actual, genuine laugh — the first one in weeks. "Mom, there's an apocalypse."
"And? That's no excuse to skip meals."
His father was standing three meters away.

David Chen had not moved during the reunion. He stood with his arms at his sides, wearing the stillness that Marcus had inherited — or maybe learned. His father's face was a mask of controlled composure, as unreadable as carved stone. He was wearing a button-down shirt, pressed, even now. Even at the end of the world, David Chen maintained his presentation.
Marcus approached him. Military bearing, straight spine. He stopped at arm's length.
His father extended his hand.

Formal. Correct. The handshake of a man who had raised his son with the understanding that emotion was something you contained, something you expressed through duty and provision rather than open affection. A handshake that honored every cultural norm of restraint passed down through generations of Chen men.
Marcus took his father's hand.
David's grip tightened.

The formal handshake became something else — his father's arm around his shoulder, pulling him in, then both arms, and David Chen was hugging his son with a desperation that violated every rule he'd ever lived by. His grip was iron. His body trembled.
"You came," his father said. The words were hoarse. Wrecked.
"I always would," Marcus answered.
They held each other for a long time. Around them, the survivors looked away, granting them the privacy that only the end of the world could afford.
The briefing took thirty minutes.

Twelve hours. That was the window. The C-130 needed to depart before dawn or risk being caught on the ground by the morning horde migration. Twelve hours to move forty-three people — some injured, some elderly, four children — from Taipei 101 to Taoyuan Airport along a route that included two kilometers of urban death zone and a terminal Marcus's team had just barely cleared.
It was going to be tight.

Marcus split the survivors into three groups, staggered departures, each escorted by a mercenary fire team. His parents would be in the second group. He'd wanted them in the first, but his mother had insisted on staying to help prep the medical patients for transport, and his father — the engineer, the planner — had immediately begun mapping secondary routes in case the primary was compromised. They were still his parents. Still stubborn. Still working.
The evacuation began.
The first group departed at 1500 hours. Eighteen able-bodied adults moving fast, carrying only what they could fight in. Kovacs led them personally.

The second group — his parents, the medical cases, the children — left at 1700. Marcus walked beside the convoy the entire way, MCX up, scanning every shadow. His mother held a little girl's hand and didn't complain once about the pace. His father carried a man with a broken leg on an improvised stretcher, his pressed shirt soaked through with sweat, and didn't slow down.
The third group left at 1930. By 2200, all forty-three survivors were loaded onto the C-130, secured in cargo net seating, and the engines were warming.
Marcus stood on the tarmac, watching the personnel board, and felt the vibration before he heard it.
The System.

Not a notification. Not a quest log update. A *pulse* — deep, resonant, like a seismic event in the architecture of his mind. The same sensation he'd felt in Chapter 55, when the notification had first flickered at the edge of his awareness, half-glimpsed and terrifying.
**[QUEST CHAIN UPDATE: The Family Reunion — Stage 3: COMPLETE. David and Mei-Ling Chen — RESCUED. Transport to New Eden in progress.]**
The gold text shimmered. He let himself feel it — the completion, the relief, the weight of a promise kept. His parents were safe. They were on the plane. They were going home.

But beneath the gold text, deeper, something else stirred. Something the System had been holding back, waiting for exactly this moment — the moment when his guard was down, when the mission was complete, when he was most vulnerable to what came next.
The Endgame Protocol.
It pulsed again. Not text. Not data. A *presence*, vast and patient, unfolding in his peripheral vision like a flower made of light and teeth.
It's time.
Marcus Chen climbed the cargo ramp, and the night swallowed him whole.
*[End of Chapter 64]*
